title: Clearing a blocked shower trap
subtitle: Remove and clean the shower tray trap or pop-up waste to restore drainage after a hair and soap-scum build-up
excerpt: How to clear a blocked shower trap. Covers locating and removing the waste cover, pulling out or unscrewing the trap body, cleaning the trap and trap cup, and reinstalling with a fresh seal.
sourceNotes: UK plumbing trade practice. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual (referenced, not reproduced). UK building trade guidance on sanitary waste systems.

glossaryTerms.definition: A shallow waste fitting below a shower tray or wet room floor drain that holds a permanent water seal to prevent sewer gas entering the room. Unlike a deep basin or bath trap, a shower trap is low-profile (typically 50 to 75 mm deep) to fit within a standard tray recess. Blocked by a mat of hair and soap-scum accumulating in the trap cup or on the cover grid. The trap body is reached by lifting or unscrewing the cover grid; the trap cup usually unscrews by hand or with a flat-bladed screwdriver.

content.text: A slow-draining or standing-water shower is almost always a

blocked with hair and soap residue. The approach is removing the trap cover, clearing the blockage from the cup, and replacing the cover with a clean water seal. A chemical drain unblocker is a short-term fix; physical removal is permanent.

Most shower tray covers have a central screw or lift out by pulling upward. Unscrew the cover with a Pozidriv screwdriver or prise the cover up with a flat-bladed screwdriver around the rim. Some pop-up traps have a bayonet fitting (push down and rotate a quarter turn. Keep the cover screw safe.

With the cover off, the trap cup is visible) a plastic or chrome cup sitting in the tray waste hole. Unscrew the cup by hand or with a flat-bladed screwdriver. Remove the mat of hair and soap-scum from inside the cup and from the tray waste hole. Flush with hot water. The blockage is rarely further down the waste pipe; if clearing the cup does not restore flow, the blockage is in the 40 mm waste run.
Clear the waste run if needed

If the blockage is in the waste run, feed the drain snake into the open waste hole and push through the 40 mm pipe to the first bend. Rotate the snake to engage the blockage and pull back. Flush with hot water to confirm flow is restored.
Refit and check the water seal

Screw the trap cup back in: hand-tight is enough; overtightening cracks plastic cups. Replace the cover. Run the shower and confirm the drain is clear. Check there is no drip from the trap body underside by reaching under the tray or inspecting through the bath panel if accessible.
